1: Josiah summons all the elders of Judah and Jerusalem. The covenant assembly — all the people from great to small — is the structural form of Sinai (Exodus 19:7-8) and the Shechem renewal (Joshua 24:1). The covenant is not made privately between God and the king; it requires the full assembly of the covenant people as witnesses and participants. The church as the covenant assembly (ekklēsia — called-out assembly) is the NT form of this gathering.
2: The king reads “all the words of the Book of the Covenant” in their ears. The covenant is orally ratified: the people must hear the words that bind them. The public reading of the covenant text is simultaneously the proclamation of its claims and the invitation to its commitment. Nehemiah 8:3: Ezra reads from early morning until midday; the people listen attentively. The NT form: “Faith comes from hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ” (Romans 10:17). The oral, public word is the means of covenant entry.
3: “The king stood beside the pillar and renewed the covenant before YHWH — to follow YHWH, keep his commandments, statutes, and decrees, with all his heart and all his soul.” The Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4-5) enacted as covenant commitment: bəkol lēḇ ûḇəkol nepeš. Josiah is the king who formally commits the covenant community to the Shema standard. Hebrews 9:15: Christ is “the mediator of a new covenant.” The old covenant’s mediation was through prophets, priests, and kings; the new covenant’s mediation is through the one who is himself prophet, priest, and king — and who embodies the Shema commitment rather than merely committing to it.
4: Josiah removes all vessels for Baal, Asherah, and the astral cults from the temple and burns them in the Kidron Valley. The systematic purge begins from the center — the temple — and moves outward. The sequence of reform: interior first, then exterior. The reformation of the church’s worship before the reformation of the culture. Jesus cleanses the temple before teaching the crowds; the inside of the cup must be cleaned first (Matthew 23:26).
5: The kᵉmārîm (pagan priests) are dismissed and the astral cults from the towns of Judah to Geba and Beersheba are defiled. The geographic scope of the reform matches the geographic scope of the apostasy: what Manasseh spread throughout the land must be addressed throughout the land. The reach of the gospel matches the reach of sin: “where sin increased, grace abounded all the more” (Romans 5:20).
6: The Asherah pole — the idol placed inside the temple by Manasseh (2 Kings 21:7) — is removed, burned, ground to dust, and scattered on graves. The five-step destruction (remove, burn, grind, scatter, defile) is total and irreversible. The thoroughness of Josiah’s desecration of the idolatrous cult objects is a type of the eschatological removal of all that is false: “the works of the devil” destroyed by the one who appeared for that purpose (1 John 3:8).
7: The quarters of the male shrine prostitutes in the temple — and the women who wove hangings for the Asherah — are torn down. The sexual cult embedded in the temple precinct is dismantled: the fertility religion that had colonized YHWH’s house is expelled from it. The sexual immorality that is always the companion of idolatry (Romans 1:24-27: YHWH gives them up to “dishonorable passions” as a consequence of idol worship) is removed together with the idols that produced it.
8: Josiah centralizes the priesthood in Jerusalem, defiling the high places from Geba to Beersheba. The Deuteronomic centralization of worship — the single place YHWH has chosen (Deuteronomy 12:5) — is finally implemented. The scattering of worship sites is the spatial form of the scattering of loyalty; centralization embodies covenant singularity. John 4:21: Jesus will abolish the geographic requirement entirely — the new covenant worship is neither at Jerusalem nor at Gerizim but in spirit and truth.
9: The priests from the high places may eat unleavened bread with their brothers but may not serve at the altar. The pastoral accommodation of existing personnel within the reform: they are maintained but their service is restricted. The reform does not execute but restricts; it finds a place for the disqualified without enabling their disqualification. The wisdom of pastoral reform that distinguishes persons from their sins.
10: Josiah defiles Topheth in the Valley of Ben-Hinnom so that no one can sacrifice children to Molech. Gê ben hinnōm — the Valley of the Son of Hinnom — will contract in later Hebrew to gêhinnōm: Gehenna. Jesus uses “Gehenna” eleven times in the Gospels to describe the place of eschatological fire (Matthew 5:22, 29-30; 2 Kings 10:28; 2 Kings 18:9; 2 Kings 23:15, 33; Mark 9:43-48; Luke 12:5). The place where children were burned becomes the image for the fire of final judgment. Josiah’s defilement of the site writes its history into its name; the name Jesus invokes is the name of an actual place where actual children died for the sin of Israel. The eschatological fire is not an abstraction but has a historical address.
11: The horses dedicated to the sun are removed from the temple entrance. The solar cult with its horse processions — ANE royal religion that Manasseh had imported — is expelled. The “horses of the sun” are the most prestigious form of astral devotion: the king’s war animals consecrated to the ruling celestial deity. The sovereignty of YHWH over the sun and stars — he created them (Genesis 1:14-18); they do not rule (Deuteronomy 4:19) — is reasserted by removing them from the temple where they had no business being.
12: Roof altars of Ahaz and the altars of Manasseh are pulled down and the dust cast into the Kidron. The undoing of two generations of accumulation: what took decades to build is demolished in the reform. The temporal asymmetry of sin and repair — sin accrues slowly, reform must work against the grain — is the condition of every revival. The gospel addresses the entire accumulation of sin at once (Colossians 2:13-14: “forgiving us all our trespasses, having canceled the record of debt”).
13: The high places Solomon built on the Mount of Corruption (Mount of Olives south ridge) — for Ashtoreth, Chemosh, Milcom — are desecrated. These shrines had stood for over three centuries since Solomon built them. The reform that reaches back to Solomon is comprehensive; Josiah is not merely addressing recent apostasy but the foundational syncretism of the monarchy’s greatest king. The Mount of Olives — the “Mount of Corruption” here — will be the place of Jesus’s agony, arrest, and ascension. What was defiled by Solomon’s apostasy will be sanctified by Christ’s obedience.
14: The sacred stones smashed, the Asherah poles cut down, the sites covered with human bones. The threefold destruction (smash, cut, defile) is irreversible: covered with bones, a site cannot be used for worship again. The radical, irreversible cleansing is the type of the judgment that will not leave “one stone upon another” (Matthew 24:2) — not merely partial reform but total demolition of what was built on the wrong foundation.
15: The Bethel altar — built by Jeroboam (1 Kings 12:28-29), the founding apostasy of the northern kingdom — is demolished after three centuries. The prophecy of the man of God from Judah (1 Kings 13:2) is fulfilled here: “a son shall be born to the house of David, Josiah by name, and he shall sacrifice on you the bones of men.” Three centuries between the word and its fulfillment demonstrates that YHWH’s prophetic word is not merely predictive but historically determinative. 2 Peter 3:8-9: “with the Lord one day is as a thousand years, and a thousand years as one day.” The words of Christ will be fulfilled with the same precision and on YHWH’s own timetable.
16: Josiah desecrates the Bethel altar with human bones taken from the hillside tombs — fulfilling exactly the 1 Kings 13:2 prophecy (“human bones shall be burned on it”). The unintentional fulfillment of the prophetic word: Josiah did not set out to fulfill a 300-year-old prophecy; he was executing a reform. YHWH works through the free, purposeful actions of free moral agents to fulfill every word he has spoken. The determinism of prophecy and the freedom of the agent are not in competition.
17: The tomb of the man of God is preserved on Josiah’s command: “Let no one disturb his bones.” The prophet whose disobedience led to his death but whose prophecy was entirely accurate has his burial honored by the king who fulfilled his prophecy. The separation between the prophet’s personal failure and the prophetic word’s reliability is preserved in Josiah’s act: the prophet was flawed; the word was not. 2 Peter 1:21: “prophecy never had its origin in the human will, but prophets, though human, spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit.”
18: Both tombs are preserved — the man of God from Judah and the Samarian prophet who buried him. The act of preservation honors the prophetic succession: the two prophets who bore witness to the Bethel judgment are jointly commemorated by the king who executed it. The community of faithful witness, even across human failure and sectarian division, is honored by the one who finally fulfills its testimony.
19: Josiah extends the reform into Samaria — former northern territory under Assyrian administration. The geographic reach of the covenant: the Davidic king acts as king over all Israel, not just Judah. The prophetic vision of the reunited twelve tribes (Ezekiel 37:15-22; Revelation 21:12) is enacted spatially by Josiah’s northward reform. Christ’s commission is explicitly unlimited: “all nations” (Matthew 28:19); the gospel that goes beyond Judah to Samaria (Acts 1:8) fulfills the Josian spatial expansion.
20: Josiah slaughters the priests of the northern high places on their own altars. The severe judgment on the agents of the northern apostasy contrasts with the milder treatment of the Judean high-place priests (v9). The greater the privilege and the longer the apostasy, the more severe the reckoning. Amos 3:2: “You only have I known of all the families of the earth; therefore I will punish you for all your iniquities.”
21: “Celebrate the Passover to YHWH your God, as written in this Book of the Covenant.” The Passover command comes at the end of the reform sequence — the purging precedes the feast. The sequence in the NT: the temple is cleansed before Jesus institutes the Lord’s Supper (Matthew 21:12-13 → 2 Kings 26:26-29). 1 Corinthians 5:7-8: “Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed. Let us therefore celebrate the festival, not with the old leaven... but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth.” The Passover restored under Josiah is the type of the Passover fulfilled in Christ.
22: “Not since the days of the judges... had any such Passover been celebrated.” The superlative Passover marks the maximum restoration of the covenant feast in Israel’s history. Yet the next Passover narrative in the NT is the one in which Jesus says “I have earnestly desired to eat this Passover with you before I suffer” (Luke 22:15) and transforms it into the meal of the new covenant. The greatest OT Passover (Josiah’s) is superseded by the one in which the Passover Lamb himself presides.
23: “In the eighteenth year of King Josiah.” The year of the book’s discovery and the year of the Passover’s restoration are the same year. The word rediscovered and the covenant feast restored are simultaneous: the recovery of Scripture and the recovery of worship are inseparable. The Reformation pattern: sola scriptura and the restoration of the Lord’s Supper to the congregation are not accidents — they belong together.
24: Josiah removes mediums, spiritists, household gods, idols, and all detestable things from Judah — the precise reversal of Manasseh’s 21:6 list. The symmetry is total: every item Manasseh introduced is removed by Josiah. The reform as comprehensive reversal of the apostasy. The NT form: the stripping away of every competing allegiance that defined the pre-conversion life (Ephesians 4:22-24: “put off your old self... be renewed... put on the new self”) — the comprehensive reversal of idolatry that characterizes genuine conversion.
25: “Neither before nor after Josiah was there a king like him who turned to YHWH with all his heart, all his soul, and all his might, according to all the Law of Moses.” The Shema (Deuteronomy 6:5) as the standard of covenantal faithfulness, applied supremely to Josiah. The superlative is immediately followed by the ʾak of v26. The greatest human embodiment of covenant faithfulness is still insufficient to undo the consequences of sin. Hebrews 10:4: “it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to take away sins” — the same impossibility applies to the most thorough reform. What is needed is not the best human performance of the covenant but the one who bears the covenant’s curse and inaugurates the new one in his blood (Hebrews 9:15).
26: “Nevertheless (ʾak), YHWH did not turn away from the burning of his great anger, which blazed against Judah because of all that Manasseh had done.” The ʾak — “nevertheless” — is the most theologically decisive single word in the book. After the greatest covenant king, after the most thorough reform, after the Passover like none before, after the Shema lived out in full: nevertheless. Manasseh’s sin is not reversible by Josiah’s obedience. The innocent blood shed under Manasseh (2 Kings 21:16) cannot be atoned by subsequent righteousness. The ʾak points forward to the only remedy that can address what Josiah could not: the blood of the new covenant, shed for the forgiveness of sins (Matthew 26:28) — the innocent blood that answers and covers all innocent blood previously shed.
27: “I will remove Judah from my sight as I removed Israel. I will reject Jerusalem.” The divine speech that cancels the election promise. But the cancellation is not final: Jeremiah 31:31-34 promises a new covenant; Ezekiel 11:17-20 promises regathering; the same prophets who announce Jerusalem’s rejection announce its restoration. The exile that follows is the crucifixion that precedes the resurrection — the apparent cancellation of the promise that is actually its fulfillment through death (John 12:24: “unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit”).
28: The standard closing formula for Josiah. The theological evaluation (vv2, 25) has already been given; the historical summary follows. The contrast between the theological record (greatest king) and the historical outcome (dies at Megiddo, followed by collapse) sets up the NT resolution: the one about whom even greater things are said (Matthew 3:17: “This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased”) also dies before the restoration comes.
29: Pharaoh Necho kills Josiah at Megiddo. The greatest covenant king dies at the hands of a foreign power that was not even targeting him — Necho was on his way to help Assyria against Babylon, not to attack Judah. The death of the righteous king through the actions of foreign political powers pursuing their own agendas is the structure of Christ’s death: Rome was pursuing its own political imperatives; Pilate found “no fault” (John 19:4); the death was not the execution of a criminal but the intersection of political convenience with divine purpose. Megiddo — Harmāgedōn in Revelation 16:16 — accumulates eschatological significance as the site of the great king’s death.
30: Josiah’s body is brought to Jerusalem and buried in his own tomb. The ʿam hāʾāreṣ who installed him (2 Kings 21:24) now mourn him and install his son. The death of the righteous king produces grief in the covenant people — Zechariah 12:11-12 describes the eschatological mourning over the one pierced as being like “the mourning for Josiah in the valley of Megiddo.” Josiah’s death at Megiddo becomes the OT’s paradigm for the grief that attends the death of the righteous king.
31: Jehoahaz: twenty-three years old, three months. The son of the best king immediately shows that greatness cannot be inherited. The institutional transmission of covenant faithfulness without the personal transformation of the heart always fails in the next generation. The three-month reign is mercy: the covenant community is not subjected to a long apostasy from the house of the greatest king.
32: “He did what was evil, just as his predecessors had done.” The reversion formula after Josiah — the greatest praise in the history of the monarchy is immediately followed by the standard condemnation. The failure of reform to create lasting faithfulness without changed hearts is the OT’s preparation for the promise of the new covenant: the law written on the heart (Jeremiah 31:33), not merely taught to the children.
33: Necho imprisons Jehoahaz at Riblah and imposes tribute. The Davidic kingdom is now a vassal state — the independence of the covenant community under its covenant king is gone. The exile has begun, functionally, before the Babylonian exile: Egyptian domination replaces Davidic sovereignty. The messianic hope intensifies precisely when the Davidic line is most diminished — the prophets who speak most clearly of the coming king (Jeremiah 23:5-6; Ezekiel 34:23-24; Zechariah 9:9) speak during and after the monarchy’s collapse.
34: Pharaoh Necho renames Eliakim (ʾĒlyāqîm — “God establishes”) as Jehoiakim (Yəhôyāqîm — “YHWH establishes”). The foreign king substitutes YHWH’s name into Judah’s king’s name as an act of ownership — he is asserting that the God of Israel’s king exists at his pleasure. The humiliation of the Davidic line continues. Yet the messianic genealogy runs through these humiliated kings: Matthew 1:11 names Jehoiakim (“Josiah the father of Jechoniah”) in the genealogy of Jesus. The Davidic line is not canceled by its humiliation; it is carried through it to its fulfillment.
35: Jehoiakim taxes the land to pay Necho’s tribute. The extraction of resources from the covenant community by the covenant king, driven by foreign tribute demands — the inversion of the king’s calling to protect and provide for the people (Deuteronomy 17:16-17 already prohibited the king from accumulating horses and wealth; now the king is accumulating tribute-obligations for a foreign master). The good king is defined by what he gives for the people; the bad king by what he takes from them.
36: Jehoiakim: twenty-five years old, eleven years. His reign spans the transition from Egyptian to Babylonian dominance (Carchemish, 605 BCE). The king who serves Egypt will find himself serving Babylon. The instability of political alliances as a substitute for covenant faithfulness: the alliance that seemed secure is overtaken by a larger power. Jeremiah 2:18: “What do you gain by going to Egypt to drink the waters of the Nile? Or what do you gain by going to Assyria to drink the waters of the Euphrates?”
37: “He did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, just as his predecessors had done.” The final verse of chapter 23 ends on the formula of condemnation — the post-Josiah collapse is complete in two verses. The last word of the chapter is ʾăḇōtāyw — “his fathers.” The ancestors’ sin has overwhelmed the son’s reform. The ʾak of v26 has worked itself out to its conclusion: Josiah’s obedience could defer but not prevent the judgment that only the new covenant’s mediator can finally absorb and reverse.
